summary: Violin Concerto No. 1 in A minor by Dmitri Shostakovich (composed 1947–48, premiered 1956 by David Oistrakh). Four movements: Nocturne, Scherzo, Passacaglia, Burlesque. Suppressed during the Zhdanov affair. Heavily uses the D-S-C-H motif (D–E♭–C–B). The work is sometimes cited as one of the first appearances of the DSCH cryptogram, alongside Symphony No. 10.

works.name: Shostakovich Violin Concerto No. 1
works.description: Violin Concerto No. 1 in A minor, Op. 77 by Dmitri Shostakovich. Composed 1947–48 but suppressed during the Zhdanov affair; premiered in 1956 by David Oistrakh (to whom it is dedicated) after Stalin's death. Four movements: Nocturne, Scherzo, Passacaglia, and Burlesque. Heavily uses the D-S-C-H motif (D–E♭–C–B, representing Shostakovich's initials in German musical notation). The third movement passacaglia is one of the most famous uses of that form in the 20th century. There is scholarly debate whether the DSCH motif's first appearance was in this concerto or in Symphony No. 10. Alfred Schnittke quoted the work in the first of his three cadenzas for Beethoven's Violin Concerto. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Shostakovich's Violin Concerto No. 1 in A minor (Op. 77) was composed in 1947–48 but had to be suppressed during the Zhdanov anti-formalism campaign. It was premiered only in 1956 by David Oistrakh, to whom it is dedicated, after Stalin's death. The four-movement concerto follows the sequence: Nocturne (slow opening), Scherzo (virtuosic second movement), Passacaglia (third movement, a ground bass form), and Burlesque (finale). The work heavily incorporates the D-S-C-H motif — D, E-flat (Es in German notation), C, B (H in German notation) — representing Shostakovich's initials. Scholars debate whether the DSCH motif appeared first in this concerto or in Symphony No. 10. Alfred Schnittke later quoted this work in the first of his three cadenzas for Beethoven's Violin Concerto. The concerto was also referenced by Krzysztof Penderecki, who used the DSCH motif in his Cadenza for Solo Violin.

In quizbowl, this work appears primarily as a contextual clue — typically named when discussing the DSCH motif, David Oistrakh, or Shostakovich's four-movement concerto form. It has not yet been a direct answerline at high difficulties on qbreader.

Concerto: third movement is a passacaglia; debate over whether the DSCH motif first appears here or in Symphony No. 10
